title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved multiple contracts and resolutions, including awarding the 2026 recycled asphalt crushing project to King Gravel for $126,900, adopting a resolution supporting state transportation funding, and directing staff to develop $100/day penalties for illegal sewer discharges. The committee also approved construction agreements for Military Road and inspection services for upcoming road projects.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC annual report

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Scoffield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project. This 2029 project will likely move up to 2028. The village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Updates were provided on Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating, Scoffield Avenue repairs by HOS Construction, and Fuller Street repairs by Melvin. A special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue was scheduled for Thursday, May 28th at 6:00 PM.

Michael reported updated modeling from Strand shows the pond will remove more phosphorus for Weston but less for Rothschild. An amendment to the agreement may be needed based on effectiveness for each community. The DOT's portion was broken out separately, with hopes the state will buy in for approximately a quarter of the cost.

Staff reported road limits were removed on April 29th. Crews performed mailbox repairs, sign repairs, and dewatering operations along Weston Avenue tributary for approximately one week. Spring curb line sweeping and patching with hot mix were completed. Storm damage cleanup was done off East Nick. Culvert failures on Jones Street required installation of an additional 18-inch culvert. Equipment maintenance included removing plow equipment, applying anti-rust fluid film, and servicing mowers and tractors.

Josh reported a service repair at Caribou Coffee and continued well maintenance. Significant clear water infiltration was identified, with April showing 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated at the wastewater plant - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water. The inflow/infiltration study with In-Pipe Technologies is upcoming. The Carrie well rehab started today with plans for a full chemical clean on the tower.

Michael presented the annual report showing net operating income of approximately $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss is at 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million of infrastructure placed in service. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers total, with 5,250 in Weston and 830 in Rothschild. The committee acknowledged the report unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supports long-term transportation funding solutions. Current state funding doesn't cover recurring costs like street maintenance and snow plowing, requiring general tax subsidies. Wisconsin remains affordable compared to neighboring states for automobile ownership. The committee voted unanimously to recommend adoption by the village board.

Michael explained the village's Ryan Street site stockpiles recycled concrete and asphalt that gets crushed into base course. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. This saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, translating to about $130,000 savings across three 2026 projects. The committee unanimously approved awarding the contract to King Gravel.

Staff sought guidance on enforcing penalties for illegal clear water discharges to the sanitary system. The municipal code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day. Committee members discussed distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations. Trustee Zagami suggested waiting for the In-Pipe Technologies study results, then sending notices with 30-60 day correction periods. The committee unanimously directed staff to create enforcement language with fines not exceeding $100 per day.

A property owner at 2230 Radkey Avenue wants to build a duplex on a lot without direct water/sewer main frontage. Staff proposed allowing a temporary long lateral connection until Radkey Avenue reconstruction. The committee added language requiring reconstruction no later than 2029, at which point the owner would pay assessment for main extension. Approved unanimously.

The agreement with Village of Rothschild covers water and sewer work on Military Road from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Steam Construction is the contractor. Weston's utility cost is $94,860 ($6,156 sanitary sewer, $88,704 water main). The water main must be relocated due to conflict with storm sewer improvements. The committee unanimously approved the agreement.

discussions.item: Construction Services for Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel Projects
discussions.body: Staff sought construction inspection services proposals. For Alderson/Jelnik intersection (roundabout), MSA bid $37,315 versus Clark Deetz at $48,910. For Bladel Avenue, MSA bid $70,400 versus Clark Deetz at $73,730. Staff recommended MSA for the roundabout and Clark Deetz for Bladel due to having full-time experienced staff rather than an intern. The committee unanimously approved both recommendations.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Recommend village board adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Direct staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines not exceeding $100/day
Recommend village board authorize Radkey Avenue temporary water/sewer connection agreement with reconstruction deadline of end of 2029
Recommend village board approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for estimated $94,860
Award Alderson/Jelnik roundabout construction inspection to MSA ($37,315)
Award Bladel Avenue construction inspection to Clark Deetz ($73,730)
Schedule Bladel Avenue special assessment meeting for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Norcon to begin Scoffield Avenue concrete repairs Wednesday/Thursday this week
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration for next meeting
Staff to investigate street lighting update for Scoffield Avenue from Birch Street to Camp Phillips Road

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting after reorganization, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield was cancelled, potentially boosting attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Work product transmittals, educational presentations, reports
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated for chair and approved unanimously. Cushing was nominated for vice chair by a commissioner, seconded by Jackson, and approved unanimously. Renee was nominated and approved unanimously as secretary.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill from Taste and Glow Balloonfest (3308 Green Pastures Lane) presented their grant request. He reported 11,000-12,000 estimated attendees last year and announced that a competing Marshfield hot air balloon event was cancelled due to permit issues, making Taste and Glow the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commissioner Hodal asked about room tracking, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year when they received $5,000. The commission requested better tracking of hotel room usage for future applications. Motion to approve $5,000 grant was made and seconded by Jackson, passing unanimously.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, including winner Anastasia, and noted the contestants toured Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese. This was the first time Marathon County hosted the event since the 1970s. The candidates expressed appreciation for the community.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow Balloonfest to provide hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with all members present voting in favor.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court Site Plan
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. The building was rotated to face east due to a gas pipeline constraint, which also reduced site fill costs. Police Chief Jeremy explained the building will accommodate 50-60 officers (currently 40) with room for future expansion to the north. Discussion addressed landscaping compliance, the alternate storage shed placement, and gas line considerations. A motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees with conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

Staff proposed a new inspection program with two annual inspections per facility in wellhead protection areas - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and a winter operational inspection. A standardized worksheet similar to DOT standards was developed. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and is closest to the village well. Commission directed staff to reach out to that facility. Motion to approve the program with reporting to both Plan Commission and Public Works passed unanimously.
Minutes from the April 13, 2026 meeting were approved unanimously with a motion and second.

Staff announced the first meeting of the joint Plan Commission-Village Board steering committee for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Discussion also touched on potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing, with staff noting Ehlers has prepared a presentation on qualifications that may be shared with the commission.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection. He asked the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project to avoid excessive taxpayer costs similar to what occurred with the Greenheck Turner building. He also requested the commission require annual reports on salt shed inspections in wellhead protection zones, citing concerns about zoning complaints that go unaddressed for years.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive recommended for approval to Board of Trustees
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with two annual inspections
Staff to report inspection findings to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg that has not gone through wellhead protection conditional use process
Joint Plan Commission-Village Board steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send email and calendar invite for steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be addressed in fall agenda

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an introductory meeting for new board members, featuring educational presentations from department heads covering the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and Aging & Disability Resource Center. The committee approved April 1st meeting minutes unanimously and received comprehensive overviews of each department's mission, services, staffing, and budget structures.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call (Supervisors Kremes and Vice Chair Rosenberg attending remotely)
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - no items for this meeting
Review of standing committee ordinances, missions, duties and responsibilities

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion passed unanimously with all members voting in favor.

Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's structure under section 2.04 sub5, covering jurisdiction over Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, North Central Health Care, and ADRC. He detailed reporting relationships with subordinate bodies including the Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, and Board of Health. Chair Boots noted the upcoming strategic plan development expected to be completed by December.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission to prevent disease, promote health, and protect from environmental hazards. The department manages over 1,200 licenses for restaurants, pools, and hotels, and operates public health vending machines with free resources including tick kits. She highlighted the Nurse Family Partnership program with first graduates, a new homeless system response position, and emergency preparedness capabilities. The department has 37.6 FTE with approximately half of its $5 million budget from tax levy.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who was unavailable. The department has nearly 140 FTE handling child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program addressing housing instability among child welfare and youth justice populations. The department's 2026 budget exceeds $22 million with approximately $7 million from tax levy.

Veteran Service Officer Aaron Galindo reported his office increased to 3.0 FTE and had 8,149 interactions in 2025, up from 5,900 the previous year. He highlighted PACT Act benefits bringing $44 million annually to local veterans and $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. The office is focusing on surviving spouse benefits, veteran housing stability through partnership with Center for Veterans Issues, and suicide prevention. He noted 30 suicides in Marathon County in 2025, seven of which were veterans.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the four-county integrated organization serving aging and disability populations. Services include information assistance, options counseling, caregiver support, dementia care specialists, falls prevention, benefit specialists, and Meals on Wheels nutrition program. He noted significant financial challenges with nutrition costs up 40-45% over four years while federal funding remained flat. The organization has about 60 FTE and 385-400 volunteers, with Marathon County contributing $413,135 in levy.
Vice Chair Rosenberg caught an error in the announced meeting date. The next meeting was corrected from Wednesday May 29th to Tuesday June 3rd at 3 PM.

actionItems: April 1st committee meeting minutes approved unanimously
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before the committee
Committee members encouraged to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with items for future agendas
Strategic plan discussion to be brought to committee in upcoming meetings
Next meeting scheduled for Tuesday, June 3rd at 3 PM

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees for new residential development, passed multiple ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and held extensive discussion about the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Acknowledgment of reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda including vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, and e-bike regulations
Discussion on remote meeting attendance policy amendments

discussions.body: Staff presented a proposal to increase park impact fees from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, consistent with the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel commented during public hearing that the village lacks real data on park maintenance costs. Trustee Motti supported the fees, noting population increases require more park space. Ordinance 26-010 passed unanimously with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami. Resolution 2026-015 passed unanimously with motion by Hermling, second by Clark.

Chief Finkey explained this creates a regional reciprocal inspection program with Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and other municipalities. When one department inspects a food truck, a sticker is placed allowing it to operate in all participating communities without additional inspections. Approved unanimously with motion by Lope, second by Zagami.

Significant debate occurred over policy language. Trustees Beck and others pushed to remove requirements that remote attendance be justified by health, safety, or welfare reasons, arguing chairs should not evaluate excuses. Trustees Hermling and Motti voted against the amendment. The board approved striking sentences requiring justification, keeping only requirements for 12-hour notice, camera use, and prohibition from closed sessions. Amendment passed with two no votes (Hermling, Motti), then original motion as amended passed with same two dissents.

discussions.item: Farmers Market Location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: Discussion-only item addressed parking concerns at new Kennedy Park location after loss of Camp Phillips site. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about cars parking on grass near vendors. Renee explained the market grew from 25 to 53 vendor spots. Mont Miller Park was discussed but rejected due to poor visibility and distance. Trustee Beck questioned costs; staff confirmed the market operates at break-even or profit through vendor fees and grants for food assistance programs. No action taken.

discussions.item: Engineering Tech Position and Outside Consultants
discussions.body: Public works director Michael explained the village has struggled to fill an engineering technician position due to limited workforce in central Wisconsin, noting even consulting firms lack local staff. Two contracts totaling approximately $108,000 for outside engineering oversight were on the agenda. Jim Pitel criticized during public comment that the administrator should be held accountable for not filling the position.

discussions.item: Secondhand Dealer License - Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC
discussions.body: Approved with amendment requiring satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit, as the business location at 5703 Jamar Street is still under construction. Motion by Hermling, second by Clark, amendment by Beck, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of Deon 52 Street spoke during public comments, criticizing the $108,000 expenditure for outside engineering consultants when an engineering technician position remains unfilled. He questioned why Lisa Beck was introduced at the SAFER meeting before official appointment, calling it inappropriate government business outside the boardroom. He also criticized lack of information on Kennedy Park in the meeting packet and called for administrator accountability. Pitel also spoke during the park impact fee public hearing, arguing the village lacks real data on park maintenance costs.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased effective immediately per Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove justification requirements
Golden Mua and Stephanie Daniels appointed to CLIPS committee
Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC secondhand dealer license approved pending background check and occupancy permit
Comprehensive plan steering committee first meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 PM
Staff to bring Weston Avenue speed limit study back to board after construction completion
Committee and commission applications to be included in board packets going forward
Intergovernmental agreement to be drafted for assisting Town of Ringle with tornado damage building inspections

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the executive committee, and unanimously approved a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster-related debris following severe weather that devastated the Ringle area. The board also approved consent agenda items and two social services budget resolutions.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Committee appointments by Chair with remarks on leadership
Election of at-large executive committee members
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fee modification for disaster relief

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs delivered remarks emphasizing leadership development, noting 34% of the board is in first or second terms. He thanked Vice Chair Boots for assistance in developing appointments and outlined goals including increasing diversity of perspectives on committees. Supervisor Cavelli moved and Supervisor Crims seconded approval of all committee appointments including county administrator appointments. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of at-large executive committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg, who cited her advocacy for children and elders), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Paper ballots were distributed with supervisors selecting two names. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.
Supervisor Cavelli moved and Supervisor Drebeck seconded approval of the consent agenda. The motion carried unanimously by voice vote.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was approved but the vote was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P and seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was approved but the vote was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fee modification for disaster relief
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented the resolution from the Solid Waste Management Board addressing severe weather damage in Ringle. The state waived its $13.10 fee, Ringle waived its $2.33 fee, and the county proposed reducing rates to $36.94 per ton (commercial rate minus state and local fees) for certified disaster debris. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special dispensation to speak, requesting the county waive all fees. Extensive discussion followed about timeline needs, insurance delays, and future policy development. The board first voted unanimously to suspend rules to hear the resolution directly, then voted unanimously to approve Resolution 26-26.

publicComment: Special dispensation was granted for two residents affected by the Ringle tornado to address the board regarding landfill tipping fees. Jessica Toasty requested Marathon County waive its fees entirely, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle are waiving their fees, and that businesses have donated $40,000+ in cleanup costs. She requested a six-month waiver. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of approximately 44 affected homeowners, noting many insurance adjusters have not yet arrived and requesting fee relief and extended timelines.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to executive committee at-large positions
Resolution 24-26 approved increasing out of home placement reserve
Resolution 25-26 approved amending social services 2026 budget
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster debris through June 17, 2025
Administrator Leonard to work with community foundation on mechanism for reimbursing landowners' unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid waste department to develop clear policy on disaster-related fee reductions within six months
Board to revisit disaster relief measures at May meeting if extension needed beyond state declaration period

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only waive reconnect fees for septic systems damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado, rejecting broader fee waivers. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing - Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing - Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation - Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation - Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation - Conservation, Planning, and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried with no opposition.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered for, against, or as interested. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and conclusions of law, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property; Pogle explained it currently has a home and horse stable, with the rezoning needed to make the home a conforming use. He confirmed no natural area would be lost. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Leur asked about tracking costs. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concern about fairness to those not in declared disaster areas and referenced the recent POT fee increase. After discussion, Supervisor Sefeld moved to forward the resolution. Supervisor Drebeck then proposed an amendment to limit the waiver only to reconnect fees for septic systems (not full system replacements or zoning fees). The amendment passed, then the amended resolution was approved with one opposing vote to forward to the county board.

Director Dave Hagen Busher presented an educational overview covering the landfill's history since 1980, the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy generating royalties since 2023, leachate treatment challenges due to PFAS regulations requiring on-site treatment by 2032, and upcoming $6 million expansion projects. He noted the landfill accepts about 1,000 tons daily.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department overview
discussions.body: County Forest Administrator Tom Lovelin presented on behalf of Director Jamie, covering the joint city-county department structure with 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees. He highlighted Nine Mile County Forest generating $3.25 million in economic impact from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld noted Tom Lovelin is retiring June 1st after 35 years. Chair Ritterder thanked him for his service.

actionItems: Forward amended fee waiver resolution to county board - waiving only reconnect fees for septic systems on tornado-damaged properties for one year
Forward Tim Veland rezoning petition (Green Valley) to county board for approval
Forward Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (McMillan) to county board for approval
Joe Tucker selected as new County Forest Administrator replacing retiring Tom Lovelin

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee approved installation of new kayak launch signs for the Plover River water trail and deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until next month when the absent chair can participate. The committee also received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising efforts and discussed graffiti removal challenges on the Oaklair River Trail.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog day at the aquatic center
Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
Topics for future meetings and staff remarks

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27, 2026 meeting were approved. A motion was made and seconded with no discussion. The motion carried unanimously.

Director Sean Osterbrink presented a timeline of the Kennedy Park master plan process as requested by the board, covering from the 2023 comprehensive outdoor recreation plan through the May 9th ribbon cutting. Scott asked clarifying questions about village capital funds and fundraising timelines. Osterbrink explained the Friends of Kennedy Park are a volunteer nonprofit currently fundraising for lights on the upper field, having approximately $84-85,000 at the community foundation toward a $117,000 goal. The acting chair defended the Friends group, noting they plan to announce fundraising totals publicly at their June tournament and deserve grace as volunteers.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and a PDF showing locations where fallen trees were removed from the river and details of wood mat installation over gas pipelines. Dan explained the mats were used to protect TC Energy (formerly ANR/ATC) pipelines during equipment access. The village now owns the mats with extras potentially available for other trail projects.

discussions.item: Dog day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to next month because Chair Katrina, who had requested the discussion, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. A resident named Andy who attended specifically to oppose allowing dogs at the public pool stated his objection: 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period. I like dogs, but it's not a place for them. We have rivers. We have lakes. That's where they belong.' He indicated he may return for next month's discussion.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
discussions.body: Staff discussed challenges removing graffiti from concrete and benches along the trail. Access limitations prevent bringing pressure washing equipment to the site. Options discussed included anti-vandalism coatings, seal coats, and volunteer work days. Committee members suggested community volunteers might help with maintenance, similar to how directors previously installed benches during a team-building day. No formal action was taken; staff will research cleaning options.

Dan from Marathon County Parks presented version 7 of informational signs for Plover River kayak launches. Three signs will be installed in Weston (Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks). Signs include maps, QR codes linking to the Kelly Station river gauge, and donation information for Marathon County Parks Foundation which funded the project through state grants. Dan noted the Highway J launch may be closed all season due to bridge replacement. Motion to approve sign installation passed unanimously.

Osterbrink reported dugout painting at Kennedy Park was completed, aquatic center pool repairs were finished, and pool filling began with opening day scheduled for June 6th. He also announced the Mock Miller Park master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler next Tuesday. The last open house for the bike and pedestrian plan is June 11th from 5-7pm.

publicComment: Two public comments were offered. Andy (6504 [street inaudible]) spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating animals should not be allowed in public pools. Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Weston), speaking online under the name 'Vern M,' criticized the 4pm meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned Kennedy Park fundraising transparency, calling the $8,700 expenditure figure 'too coincidental' and asking about the location of grant money and glow event proceeds. He suggested the Friends of Kennedy Park may be 'frauds' for not sharing detailed financials.
actionItems: Dog day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Yellow Banks kayak launch signs approved for installation at three Weston locations
Staff to research graffiti removal and anti-vandalism coating options for Oaklair River Trail
Staff to bring food allergy awareness playground signage proposal to future meeting
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Next Parks Committee meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee held a routine meeting featuring an educational presentation on the Municipal Clerk's office budget and operations. The committee acknowledged monthly financial reports and learned about election administration, licensing, tax collection, and the costs associated with the village newsletter. Committee member Evan Eberle announced his resignation as he is moving out of the village.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports for April
Staff remarks and farmers market discussion

discussions.body: Motion to approve minutes from the previous Finance and HR meeting was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. The motion passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: TIFF one and TIFF two reports
discussions.body: Jessica provided an update on TIF district closure, noting they anticipate finalizing TIF 1 in October-November. The bonds are due October 1st, and the CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds. A committee member asked about final numbers, and Jessica confirmed they will be able to pay off the debt. Items 5, 6, and 7 were acknowledged together on a motion by Daniels, seconded by Soaper, passing unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented an overview of her department's budget and operations. The office has three staff members (two full-time, one part-time) down from four full-time plus two assistants previously. She detailed election administration costs noting Badger Books cost about $11,000 for four units, and postage increased from $6,000 to $15,000 due to fall election absentee ballot mailings. The 2024 presidential election saw over 800 registrations and over 600 in-person absentee voters on the first day. Chief inspector wages are below average at $13/hour compared to county average of $15, which Pam plans to address in the next election term.

Pam explained the pet licensing fee increase from $10 to $20 for neutered dogs, which was requested by Marathon County to help cover their $100,000 annual payment to the Humane Society. The non-dog stray contract was discussed, with committee members noting that eight mice cost $175 each to process through the Humane Society. A suggestion was made to require law enforcement to bring in strays rather than allowing residents to do so directly.

The quarterly newsletter 'The Wire' costs approximately $30,000 annually ($9,000 postage, $20,000 printing) to reach 5,089 postal patrons. Committee members questioned whether costs could be reduced through bidding or email distribution. Staff noted they previously tried eliminating mailed newsletters but residents requested they return. The village also sends a weekly e-newsletter to about 1,500 subscribers with approximately 50% open rate.

Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well over the first three Saturdays. She noted some weather challenges on Tuesdays with wind affecting vendors. The market is full with food trucks, coffee vendors, craft vendors, and some early produce (lettuce, radishes, rhubarb). A committee member raised concerns about parking on Alderson and Newport streets. Staff clarified parking is restricted on the east side of Alderson but allowed on the west side and both sides of Newport.

Evan Eberle announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. He thanked the committee for the opportunity to serve. The chair noted there are now open spots on this and other committees and encouraged members to recruit interested citizens.

actionItems: Minutes from previous meeting approved
April budget status report, TIFF reports, and legal details report acknowledged
CDA resolution needed this summer to call TIF 1 bonds before October 1st deadline
Clerk plans to propose increasing chief inspector wages to $15/hour for next election term
Committee seeking citizen volunteers to fill open committee positions
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to Chapter 50 nuisances, recommended an e-bike ordinance with added emoto restrictions, discussed overnight parking regulations without taking action, conditionally approved a crosswalk near Mount Olive Church, adopted fire inspection requirements for mobile food vendors, approved address corrections, and authorized demolition of an abandoned structure. A resident raised concerns about the new farmers market location and various code enforcement issues.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Acknowledgment of department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Overnight parking regulations October 31st to May 1st
Crosswalk request on Alderson Street near Mount Olive Church
Mobile food vendors fire inspection requirements

discussions.body: Code Enforcement Officer Audrey noted that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the enforcement list after the property sold at auction and the new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), issued 281 citations, and made 410 traffic stops. The department has hired its 40th officer and during summer, two SROs are assigned to zoning violations and pet licensing enforcement. Motion to acknowledge reports passed unanimously.

The committee finalized amendments that had been discussed in previous meetings, including switching tree limb height requirements to 14 and 10 feet over streets and walkways, and adding language that properties must comply with the jurisdiction that has authority. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously.

The committee discussed the model e-bike ordinance for Chapter 82. Chief Hunt strongly recommended prohibiting emotos (electric dirt bike-style vehicles) from roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property due to safety concerns, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. The committee debated whether to include exemptions for children's electric scooters but decided to rely on officer discretion. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend approval with the addition of emoto restrictions passed unanimously. Rothschild has not yet approved theirs, Schofield declined, and Wausau is making adjustments.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour no parking window (4am-4pm) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities, which typically have 4-5 hour windows overnight. Michael Wodalski from Public Works suggested the even/odd restrictions help with daytime snow removal but acknowledged most plowing doesn't reach residential streets until 7-8am anyway. Chief Hunt preferred keeping overnight restrictions to give officers time to clear cars before morning plowing. The committee discussed potentially having six to eight problem areas signed specifically. No action was taken; Michael will prepare specific exception locations for the July meeting.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between their property and DC Everest school district parking. The committee discussed that church services generate significant traffic but no accidents have been reported. Staff estimated a basic crosswalk would cost approximately $2,000, while a lighted system would be $25,000-$30,000. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend approval to the board contingent on Mount Olive covering the installation costs passed unanimously. Michael Wodalski will contact Mount Olive to determine their willingness to pay.

Fire Marshal Marty presented a regional fire inspection program involving Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and SAFER. Vendors would be inspected once annually and receive a sticker valid for all participating municipalities, eliminating duplicate inspections. Chief Hunt noted that Wausau declined to participate, preferring their own system. Fire Marshal Marty already performed one inspection that failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane cylinders. Motion by Chwanka, seconded by Campkey, to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Staff presented three address correction requests: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses, owners already agreed), 3913/3917 Ross Avenue (out of sequence addresses with apartment discovered during sale), and 2211 Radkey Avenue (new duplex on private drive with incorrectly sequenced apartment complex). Chief Hunt also identified Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski as having addressing issues with eight separate addresses for one structure. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Staff requested authorization to begin razing an abandoned structure that has been vacant since 2010-2011 and posted since 2021. A previous fire department training burn was cancelled because the structure was too deteriorated to be safe. Staff discovered chickens were being housed inside during winter. The current owners prefer the raze order process, and demolition costs will be added to their tax roll. The property owners are current on taxes. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend authorization passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke expressing concerns about the new farmers market location, predicting the park's grassy areas will be destroyed by parking vehicles on grass. He questioned whether current village code prohibits grass parking and suggested ADA-compliant parking spaces should be on asphalt rather than grass. He also questioned the origin of new fire inspections for food trucks, asked about food truck permitting on public property, and criticized the age of some code enforcement cases, stating some have shown no visible progress after a year.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to Board of Trustees for approval
Recommend e-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions to Board of Trustees for approval
Michael Wodalski to prepare list of specific locations requiring even/odd parking signage for July meeting
Michael Wodalski to contact Mount Olive Church regarding willingness to pay for crosswalk installation
Recommend Ordinance 26-8 (mobile food vendor fire inspections) to Board of Trustees
Recommend address corrections at Delicoski, Ross, and Radkey to Board of Trustees
Investigate addressing issues at Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski Street
Authorize staff to begin raze order process for 4104 Shore Avenue
Investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster outside
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 6th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.

Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects
